mappings.rationaleAI: Plate tectonics covers seafloor spreading, mid-ocean ridges, subduction creating trenches, mountain building, and the relationship between plate boundaries and volcanic/seismic activity -- fundamental to understanding all major oceanic and continental surface features.
The Mariana Trench is the deepest oceanic trench, formed through Pacific Plate subduction, directly exemplifying deep-sea trenches and oceanic deeps as major features of the ocean floor.

Volcanology covers shield, stratovolcano, and caldera types at subduction zones, mid-ocean ridges, and hotspots, directly relevant to volcanic mountains and the worldwide system of mountain ranges and volcanic island arcs.
Oceanography covers the geology of the ocean floor including mid-ocean ridges and hydrothermal vents, and the mapping of oceanic physiography from the Challenger expedition onward.

Britain's geological formation spans Precambrian shield rocks, Paleozoic collisions joining landmasses, and sedimentary processes, illustrating continental physiography and the age and structure of shield areas.
Antarctica's unique geography including its ice sheets and continental features contributes directly to the physiography of the Antarctic continent.
The Challenger Expedition mapped ocean floors and discovered the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, foundational to understanding oceanic ridges and the physiography of ocean basins.
Earth's core structure including core-mantle interactions and dynamo theory connects to the oceanic crust and the deep processes underlying ocean basin formation.
Coral reef formation through calcium carbonate deposition over millennia directly maps to coral islands, coral reefs, and atolls as physical features of the Earth's surface.
